title: Why I stopped seeing the river I cross
summary: Reflect on something you pass every day and no longer notice, and what made you see it again.
topic: Something you pass daily and stopped noticing
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences about something you pass every day and have stopped noticing, such as a river, a building, or a tree. Explain how it became invisible to you and what made you look again. Use present simple, past simple, and the connectors 'until' and 'because'.
hintStarters: I cross the same river twice a day, but for years I never really…
Somewhere along the way it turned into part of the background, like…
Then one morning, because the bus stopped on the bridge, I…
Now I try to look at it on purpose, although it is hard because…
sampleText: I cross the same river in Porto twice a day, on the bus to work and on the bus home. For the first few months I looked at it every time. The water changed colour with the weather, and once I saw a man rowing a small boat in the rain. But slowly I stopped seeing it. It became part of the background, like the seat in front of me or the noise of the engine. I started reading my phone on the bridge instead, until I could not have told you what colour the water was that morning. This went on for almost two years. Then one day the bus stopped in the middle of the bridge because of an accident ahead. We waited for ten minutes with nothing to do. I looked up, and the river was full of fog, and a single bird stood on a post in the water. It was beautiful, and I felt strange, because I crossed this view four times a day and never saw it. I had taken the whole river for granted. Now I try to look at it on purpose, at least once a day. It is harder than it sounds, because attention slips away so easily. But I would rather see an ordinary river than see nothing at all.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: băng qua cùng một con sông

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a man rowing a small boat in the rain
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một người đàn ông chèo chiếc thuyền nhỏ trong mưa

sampleAnnotations.phrase: It became part of the background
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nó trở thành một phần của phông nền

sampleAnnotations.phrase: reading my phone on the bridge
what colour the water was that morning
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nước có màu gì sáng hôm đó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the bus stopped in the middle of the bridge
the river was full of fog
a single bird stood on a post
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một con chim duy nhất đứng trên một cái cọc

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I had taken the whole river for granted
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tôi đã xem cả con sông là điều hiển nhiên
nhìn nó một cách có chủ đích
sự chú ý trôi đi quá dễ dàng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: see an ordinary river than see nothing at all
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nhìn thấy một con sông bình thường còn hơn chẳng thấy gì

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Until' to mark the end point of a situation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'I started reading my phone on the bridge instead, until I could not have told you what colour the water was' dùng 'until' để chỉ thời điểm một tình trạng kéo dài đến đó rồi dừng hoặc thay đổi. 'Until' nối hành động với điểm kết thúc của nó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'I started reading my phone on the bridge instead, until I could not have told you what colour the water was' uses 'until' to mark the point where a situation that lasted reaches its end or changes. 'Until' links an action to its end point.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect for a realisation about earlier time
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'I had taken the whole river for granted' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành để nói về điều đã xảy ra suốt một thời gian trước khoảnh khắc nhận ra trên cầu. Nó lùi về trước thời điểm chính trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'I had taken the whole river for granted' uses the past perfect to describe something that had been true over time before the moment of realisation on the bridge. It steps back before the main past moment.

sampleTranslationVi: Tôi băng qua cùng một con sông ở Porto hai lần mỗi ngày, trên xe buýt đi làm và trên xe buýt về nhà. Trong vài tháng đầu, tôi nhìn nó mỗi lần. Nước đổi màu theo thời tiết, và có lần tôi thấy một người đàn ông chèo chiếc thuyền nhỏ trong mưa. Nhưng dần dần tôi ngừng nhìn thấy nó. Nó trở thành một phần của phông nền, như chiếc ghế trước mặt hay tiếng động cơ. Tôi bắt đầu đọc điện thoại trên cầu thay vào đó, đến mức tôi không thể nói cho bạn biết nước có màu gì sáng hôm đó. Chuyện này kéo dài gần hai năm. Rồi một ngày xe buýt dừng giữa cầu vì có tai nạn phía trước. Chúng tôi đợi mười phút mà chẳng có gì làm. Tôi ngước lên, và con sông phủ đầy sương, và một con chim duy nhất đứng trên một cái cọc trong nước. Cảnh đó đẹp, và tôi thấy lạ, vì tôi băng qua khung cảnh này bốn lần mỗi ngày mà chưa từng thấy nó. Tôi đã xem cả con sông là điều hiển nhiên. Bây giờ tôi cố nhìn nó một cách có chủ đích, ít nhất một lần mỗi ngày. Việc đó khó hơn nghe tưởng, vì sự chú ý trôi đi quá dễ dàng. Nhưng tôi thà nhìn thấy một con sông bình thường còn hơn chẳng thấy gì cả.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about crossing the river daily.
options: I cross the same river twice a day.
I cross the same river two times of day.
I crosses the same river twice a day.
I cross the same river twice in day.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Twice a day' is the natural phrase. 'Two times of day' is clumsy; 'crosses' disagrees with 'I'; 'twice in day' needs 'a'.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase is natural for frequency: 'twice a day' or 'two times of day'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the most natural sentence about the river becoming invisible.
options: It became part from the background.
It became part of the background.
It become part of the background.
It became a part of background.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Became part of the background' is correct. 'Part from' is wrong; 'become' should be past 'became'; 'part of background' drops 'the'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence using 'until' to mark an end point.
options: I read my phone since I could not say what colour the water was.
I read my phone until I could not said what colour the water was.
I read my phone until I could not say what colour the water was.
I read my phone until I could not say what colour was the water.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Until' marks the end point and 'what colour the water was' uses statement order. 'Since' is wrong here; 'could not said' uses the wrong form; option 4 inverts the indirect question.
mcQuestions.hint: In the indirect question, does the word order invert like a direct question?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence about how long it lasted.
options: This went on since almost two years.
This went on for almost two year.
This goes on for almost two years.
This went on for almost two years.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'For almost two years' is correct. 'Since' is wrong with a duration; 'two year' should be plural; 'goes on' is present, not past.
mcQuestions.hint: Do we use 'for' or 'since' with a length of time like 'two years'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence using 'because' for the reason the bus stopped.
options: The bus stopped because of an accident ahead.
The bus stopped because an accident ahead.
The bus stopped, so an accident ahead.
The bus stopped because of an accident is ahead.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because of + noun phrase' is correct. 'Because an accident ahead' lacks a verb; 'so' shows result; option 4 adds a wrong verb after 'because of'.
mcQuestions.hint: Before a noun phrase, do we use 'because' or 'because of'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the most natural sentence describing the bird.
options: A single bird standed on a post in the water.
A single bird stood on a post in the water.
A single bird stood on a post on the water.
A single bird stood in a post in the water.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stood on a post in the water' is correct. 'Standed' is wrong; 'on the water' changes the sense; 'in a post' uses the wrong preposition.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the past tense of 'stand'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best sentence about crossing the view but never seeing it.
options: I crossed this view four times a day and never see it.
I crossed this view four times a day and never seen it.
I crossed this view four times a day and never saw it.
I crossed this view four times in day and never saw it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Crossed' and 'saw' (past simple) match. 'See' is present; 'never seen' needs 'had'; 'four times in day' needs 'a'.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the past simple of 'see'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the correct past-perfect sentence about taking it for granted.
options: I had took the whole river for granted.
I have taken the whole river for granted then.
I had taken the whole river for grant.
I had taken the whole river for granted.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had taken … for granted' is correct. 'Had took' uses the wrong participle; 'have … then' is the wrong tense pairing; 'for grant' is not the idiom.
mcQuestions.hint: The past participle of 'take' is 'took' or 'taken'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about looking on purpose.
options: Now I try to look at it on purpose.
Now I try to look it on purpose.
Now I try looking at it by purpose.
Now I try to look at it on the purpose.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Look at it on purpose' is correct. 'Look it' drops 'at'; 'by purpose' is wrong; 'on the purpose' adds a wrong article.
mcQuestions.hint: Which is the fixed phrase: 'on purpose' or 'by purpose'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best closing sentence about preferring to see something.
options: I would rather to see an ordinary river than see nothing at all.
I would rather see an ordinary river than see nothing at all.
I would rather see an ordinary river that see nothing at all.
I would rather seeing an ordinary river than see nothing at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would rather see … than see' uses base verbs correctly. Option 2 adds 'to'; option 3 uses 'that' for 'than'; option 4 uses '-ing' after 'rather'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'would rather', do we use the base verb or 'to + verb'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do the things we see most often become the hardest to truly notice? Is it possible to choose what we pay attention to, or does our mind decide for us?
